title: AWS Cloud Architecture for Beginners
subtitle: How AWS actually works — regions, services, networking, security, and the patterns that make cloud architectures durable
description: A practical course on Amazon Web Services for engineers and technical practitioners new to cloud. Covers the core mental model (regions, AZs, the shared-responsibility model), the workhorse services (EC2, S3, RDS, Lambda, VPC), networking and security, architecture patterns (multi-tier apps, event-driven, serverless, microservices), the Well-Architected Framework, and cost optimization. Built from AWS official documentation, AWS whitepapers, and standard cloud architecture references. Practical, focused on what you'll actually use day-to-day rather than encyclopedic service coverage.
audience: Developers, engineers, founders, and curious learners new to AWS or to cloud in general. Some basic technical familiarity (you know what a server is) helps but no AWS background required.
prerequisites: Basic technical familiarity (you know what a server, database, and DNS are)
Comfort with reading configuration and command-line examples
outcomes: Explain AWS's global infrastructure (regions, AZs, edge locations) and what it means for your architecture
Use the core services (EC2, S3, RDS, Lambda, VPC) confidently
Design networking and security with VPC, security groups, IAM
Recognize and apply standard cloud architecture patterns
Apply the Well-Architected Framework principles to design choices
Reason about cost across compute, storage, and data transfer
Avoid the common AWS mistakes that produce surprise bills and outages
